verses.text: Hannah's prayer turns to song: 'My heart rejoiceth in the LORD.' Once weeping, she now exults — not merely in a son, but in God Himself. 'There is none holy as the LORD.' Answered prayer should lift our eyes past the gift to the Giver, who alone is holy.

Hannah's prayer turns to song: 'My heart rejoiceth in the LORD.' Once weeping, she now exults — not merely in a son, but in God Himself. 'There is none holy as the LORD.' Answered prayer should lift our eyes past the gift to the Giver, who alone is holy.

Hannah's prayer turns to song: 'My heart rejoiceth in the LORD.' Once weeping, she now exults — not merely in a son, but in God Himself. 'There is none holy as the LORD.' Answered prayer should lift our eyes past the gift to the Giver, who alone is holy.

Her song celebrates a God who turns the world upside down: the strong are broken, the weak girded with strength, the hungry filled while the full beg bread. 'He raiseth up the poor out of the dust.' The LORD delights to lift the lowly and to humble the proud.

'He will keep the feet of his saints,' for no one prevails by strength alone. Hannah ends looking far ahead: the LORD will judge the earth and 'exalt the horn of his anointed.' From a mother's joy springs a prophecy of the King, the LORD's Anointed, yet to come.

'He will keep the feet of his saints,' for no one prevails by strength alone. Hannah ends looking far ahead: the LORD will judge the earth and 'exalt the horn of his anointed.' From a mother's joy springs a prophecy of the King, the LORD's Anointed, yet to come.

Samuel stays to minister before the LORD under Eli, while Eli's own sons are 'sons of Belial' who 'knew not the LORD.' A child given to God thrives where grown priests rot. Nearness to holy things is no substitute for actually knowing the God they serve.

Samuel stays to minister before the LORD under Eli, while Eli's own sons are 'sons of Belial' who 'knew not the LORD.' A child given to God thrives where grown priests rot. Nearness to holy things is no substitute for actually knowing the God they serve.

Eli's sons seize the worshippers' offerings by force and treat the sacrifice with contempt, so that 'men abhorred the offering of the LORD.' Greed in God's house drives people away from worship. To handle holy things for selfish gain is among the gravest of sins.

Samuel ministers in a little linen coat his mother brings him each year, and for her gift God grants Hannah more children. The son she gave away was no loss; the LORD is no man's debtor, and He repays the open hand far beyond what it ever surrendered.

Old Eli hears of his sons' evil and rebukes them feebly, but they will not listen, 'because the LORD would slay them.' A father who indulged sin too long can no longer turn it. Soft, late correction is no correction; persistent sin can harden the heart past hearing.

Meanwhile 'the child Samuel grew on, and was in favour both with the LORD, and also with men' — the very words later written of the boy Jesus. Quiet, godly growth shines all the brighter against the dark backdrop of Eli's failing house.

A man of God brings Eli God's word: 'them that honour me I will honour, and they that despise me shall be lightly esteemed.' Eli had honoured his sons above God. Here is a principle that governs every life — God will be honoured, and He honours those who honour Him.

The sentence falls on Eli's house: its strength will be cut off and the priesthood pass from it. Privilege presumed upon becomes privilege removed. No sacred office is held by birthright alone; God will not be bound to a family that despises Him.

The sign is given — both sons will die on a single day — and God promises to raise up 'a faithful priest' who will do His will. Even in judgment, mercy looks ahead. The LORD removes the unfaithful, but always keeps for Himself a faithful servant to come.
